title: Migrate legacy payment processor to Stripe API with zero-downtime cutover
author: rayan
description:
Migrates the legacy in-house payment processor to Stripe's Payment Intents API. Handles ~$2M/day in transaction volume across 14 markets. Cutover was zero-downtime: dual-write window of 72hrs, shadow-traffic comparison job, and a feature-flagged rollback path. Includes idempotency-key plumbing through the existing fraud-scoring pipeline so we don't double-charge on retries.

Key decisions:
- Kept the fraud rules engine in-process (not as a side-car) to preserve <50ms p99 added latency.
- Wrapped Stripe webhook handlers in our existing event-bus consumer pattern so downstream teams (Risk, Ledger) didn't have to change anything.
- Added a kill-switch on `payments.stripe_enabled` config so we can fail back to legacy in <30s.
review_comments: jonas: nice work on the dual-write window. one nit: the shadow-comparison job should emit a metric for tail-latency divergence, not just mean. otherwise you'll miss the long tail when you cut over.
rayan: good call, added p95/p99 emission in 9b3a2f1. also added an alarm at >20% divergence.

title: Reduce fraud-rules query latency by 40% via composite indexes + plan hints
author: rayan
description:
The fraud-rules evaluation path was hitting a sequential scan on `transactions(merchant_id, created_at)` for high-volume merchants. EXPLAIN ANALYZE showed the planner was rejecting the partial index because of a stale histogram on `created_at`. Fixed by:
- Adding a composite index on `(merchant_id, created_at DESC) INCLUDE (amount, currency)` to cover the hot read path.
- Manual `ANALYZE` cadence for the 8 highest-volume merchants every 15 min (cron).
- Removed an unused trigger that fired on every row insert and was dragging write throughput.

Result: p99 query time on the fraud-rules read path dropped from 142ms → 84ms (-40%). Daily transaction volume on the new system is steady at $2M+ with zero rule-evaluation timeouts in the last 7 days.
review_comments: priya: the INCLUDE columns help here but watch the index size — it'll grow with `transactions`. Worth a partition strategy in Q1.
rayan: agreed. opened FRAUD-127 to track partitioning by month.

title: Add idempotency-key replay protection to fraud-scoring API gateway
author: rayan
description:
Closes FRAUD-89. Some upstream callers (notably the mobile checkout team) were retrying on 5xx without idempotency keys, which could re-trigger scoring on the same transaction and inflate decline rates. Added a small Redis-backed dedup layer in front of the API gateway with a 24hr TTL keyed on `(merchant_id, transaction_id, attempt_id)`.

Docs updated in `docs/api/fraud-scoring.md`. Wrote a short RFC at `docs/rfcs/0014-idempotency.md` first to align with the Risk team.

title: Internal Developer Portal: surface fraud-platform service catalog
author: rayan
description:
Adds the Fraud Platform team's owned services (fraud-scoring-api, rules-engine, payment-gateway, ledger-sync) to the internal Developer Portal so on-call rotations and dependency owners are discoverable from one place. Pulled service metadata from our existing service.yaml files; added a small Next.js route `/teams/fraud-platform`.

This was off my normal area but the Platform Eng team was bandwidth-limited and Sarah (their EM) asked if I'd help land it. Worth doing — half the incidents in the last quarter wasted 10+ minutes on 'who owns this'.
